front: Play: the protagonist bets he will not tell the passing moment 'tarry awhile, thou art so fair'
Play: the female lead sings 'My peace is gone, my heart is sore' at her spinning wheel, inspiring Schubert's 'Gretchen am Spinnrade'
Play: ends by invoking the 'eternal feminine' that 'draws us on high'
Play: the title scholar dances with witches on Walpurgis Night and encounters a homunculus
Play: in Part II, the title character has a child with Helen of Troy
Play: the female lead sings 'The King in Thule,' in which a golden goblet is tossed into the tide at a king's death
Play: includes a prologue in the theater and a prologue in Heaven
Play: Simon Magus appears on paper money in Part II
Play: Delacroix published lithographs illustrating it in 1828; Harry Clarke made Art Nouveau illustrations in 1925
Play: Hugo Wolf wrote 51 art songs based on the author's texts, opening with three settings of 'The Harp Player'
Play: Mahler's Symphony of a Thousand uses texts from Part II for the holy anchorites sung by chorus
Novel: the protagonist cries when the woman he loves says the name 'Klopstock'
back: The Sorrows of Young Werther (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Novel: the protagonist bonds with the woman he loves over their mutual appreciation of Goldsmith and Klopstock
back: The Sorrows of Young Werther (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Novel: Friedrich Nicolai parodied this work by adding chicken's blood to the suicide scene
back: The Sorrows of Young Werther (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Novel: the protagonist receives a pink ribbon in the mail
back: The Sorrows of Young Werther (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Poem: the title figure promises 'my daughters shall tend thee with sisterly care' to a boy riding on horseback with his father
Poem: the father dismisses his son's fears by saying ''tis the aged grey willows deceiving thy sight' and 'it's a streak of fog'
Poem: Schubert's setting opens with G octaves in the piano's right hand
Work: the first poem commands 'Tell me you stones, O speak, you towering palaces!' in a cycle of 24 sensual poems originally titled Erotica
work: Italian Journey / Roman Elegies / Venetian Epigrams

front: Work: Tischbein painted the author reclining on a rock slab 'in the Roman Campagna' next to a relief of Iphigenia's sacrifice
work: Italian Journey / Roman Elegies / Venetian Epigrams

front: Work: a travelogue with the epigraph 'I, too in Arcadia,' recounting a 1786-88 journey
work: Italian Journey / Roman Elegies / Venetian Epigrams

front: Work: the fourth poem imagines people questioning the author about a suicidal character from another novel
work: Italian Journey / Roman Elegies / Venetian Epigrams

front: Work: dedicated to Christiane Vulpius; Angelica Kauffman painted a portrait of the author in Rome during this period
work: Italian Journey / Roman Elegies / Venetian Epigrams

front: Work: inspired by Joseph Hammer-Purgstall's translation of Hafez and an affair with Marianne von Willemer; uses ghazals
Work: features conversations between the lovers Hatem and Suleika; each of its twelve books has a Persian and German title
Novel: the romantic attractions between two couples are explained through chemical double-displacement reactions; Captain Otto and Ottilie are invited to a Weimar home
Novel: a woman drops her baby cousin while pushing a rowboat into a lake, causing the baby to drown
Novel: inspired by an aphorism about people who 'mix like water and wine' or 'separate like water and oil'; Walter Benjamin wrote an essay on it
Novel: the protagonist plays Hamlet in an acting troupe after being rejected by Mariane, and eventually joins the Tower Society
back: Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Novel: the character Mignon sings 'Do you know the land where the lemon trees bloom?'
back: Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Novel: Mignon also sings 'None but the Lonely Heart' ('Nur wer die Sehnsucht kennt')
back: Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Novel: Mignon is first introduced as a child purchased from a theatre troupe; she later performs an egg dance
back: Wilhelm Meister's Apprenticeship (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Poem: 'Over all hilltops is peace... in all the treetops / hearest thou / hardly a breath'; ends with 'soon thou too shalt rest'
Poem: the author inscribed the lines of the second poem on the walls of a cabin
Poem: written after a 73-year-old author proposed to 17-year-old Ulrike von Levetzow and was rejected; forms the second part of a 'Trilogy of Passion'
Poem: contains the lines 'They urged me to those lips, with rapture crowned / Deserted me, and hurled me to the ground'
Poem: a magic broom is instructed to fetch pails of water and floods a workshop; the apprentice futilely cuts the broom in half
back: The Sorcerer's Apprentice (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Poem: source of the idiom 'the spirits that I called'; set to music by Paul Dukas
back: The Sorcerer's Apprentice (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Work: an anti-Newtonian treatise; associated with a triangle of nine segments using labels like 'unnecessary'
back: Theory of Colors (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Work: Wittgenstein's Remarks on Colour expands on ideas from this treatise; Turner referenced it in a painting about the morning after the flood
back: Theory of Colors (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Work: posits a primordial 'Urpflanze' from which all plant life developed
back: The Metamorphosis of Plants (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Poem: a moth consumed by a flame; speaker writes 'You remain no longer caught / In the penumbral gloom'
Poem: a temple prostitute is deified for committing sati after discovering an avatar of Shiva in her bed
back: The God and the Bayadere (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Poem: ends with 'yet, to be loved, what happiness! / What happiness, ye gods, to love!' after describing a horseback ride to a nighttime tryst
back: Welcome and Farewell (Johann Wolfgang von Goethe)

front: Poem: a boy picks a rosebud despite its promise to prick him
Play: ends with Freedom visiting Klarchen's lover in jail; Beethoven composed the overture
Author: called out 'more light! more light!' on his deathbed
Author: secretary Eckermann recorded nine years of his table talk; he quipped 'architecture is frozen music' and advanced a notion of the 'Daemonic'
Author: declared 'the classical I call healthy, and the romantic sickly'
Author: coined the phrase 'the epoch of world literature is at hand' while comparing a Chinese novel to one of his own works
Author: defined a novella as telling of a 'single remarkable event'
Author: during an interview with Napoleon, Pierre Daru mentioned his translation of Voltaire's Mahomet, and Napoleon responded 'That is an awful play'
Author: first published Diderot's Rameau's Nephew in a German translation
Author: a pioneer of the Innerlichkeit style; worked for the Duke of Saxe-Weimar, who ennobled him
Author: wrote the story 'The Green Snake and the Beautiful Lily,' informed by an interest in alchemy
Author: a section of Nabokov's 'Pale Fire' quotes him after the line 'And now what shall I do? My knight is pinned'
